prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes a fourfold-value fine for a very specific list of grocery adulterations — pepper mixed with stone fragments, salt mixed with earth, and asafoetida mixed with tree resin — placed alongside the related rule that ghee sold under the name of ghee but adulterated with sesame oil (or oil with ghee) likewise incurs a fourfold penalty on the merchant? Quote the two verses listing these adulterations verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The exact triplet 'pepper with stone fragments, salt with earth, asafoetida with tree resin' — paired with the ghee-and-sesame-oil rule and the fourfold-value penalty — is a signature commercial-fraud catalogue unique to this smṛti; no other smṛti in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Viṣṇu, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa) names asafoetida-with-resin or pepper-with-stone-fragments as adulteration species, and the word 'asafoetida' appears nowhere else in the corpus.

prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus declares that for the convergence of all sins together no ordinary penance is known, and that the prescribed remedy in such a case is to take up the skull-bowl and observe the Kāpālika vow — explicitly named by the wise as the ultimate penance — even though for each single sin committed alone the wise know a separate penance? Quote the paired verses on the convergence of sins and the skull-bowl vow verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The specific doctrinal pairing — that no penance exists when all sins converge, and that the skull-bowl Kāpālika vow is the explicit ultimate remedy — is unique to this smṛti; the strings 'skull-bowl', 'Kāpālika vow', and 'all sins converge' do not appear in any other corpus work (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Viṣṇu, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa, Vīramitrodaya).

prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes that the king should banish from the kingdom a Brahmin who has abandoned his sacred fire by first smearing the man's body with ashes and fumigating him with the hair and bones of a corpse — and, in an immediately adjacent verse, makes 'the man who violates convention' go naked after being fanned with the hair of a corpse and his head adorned with a garland of corpse-hair? Quote both verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This precise pair of degradation rituals — body smeared with ashes plus fumigation with corpse-hair-and-bones for the abandoner of the sacred fire, then fanning with corpse-hair plus a corpse-hair head-garland for the convention-violator who is sent away naked — is unique to this smṛti; the words 'fanned' and 'fumigated with hair' in this purificatory-banishment sense do not appear in any other corpus smṛti, and no other dharmaśāstra in the collection clusters these specific corpse-hair humiliations together.
